questions.q: Nana Saheb led the rebellion of 1857 in which city?
questions.explanation: Nana Saheb was the principal leader of the 1857 uprising at Kanpur (then Cawnpore).

questions.q: Nana Saheb was the adopted son of which last Peshwa?
questions.explanation: He was adopted by Baji Rao II, the last Peshwa of the Maratha Confederacy.

questions.q: Nana Saheb rebelled largely because the British denied him what?
questions.explanation: The British refused to continue the pension that had been granted to his adoptive father Baji Rao II.

questions.q: To which dynasty's lineage did Nana Saheb's title 'Peshwa's heir' connect him?
questions.explanation: As heir to the Peshwa, Nana Saheb was tied to the Maratha tradition of leadership.

questions.q: In which year did Nana Saheb lead the revolt at Kanpur?
questions.explanation: The Kanpur rebellion under Nana Saheb broke out in 1857.

questions.q: What was Nana Saheb's personal name before he was known by the title Nana Saheb?
questions.explanation: Nana Saheb's given name was Dhondu Pant.

questions.q: What is said about Nana Saheb's fate after the revolt was suppressed?
questions.explanation: Nana Saheb disappeared after the revolt and his ultimate fate has never been confirmed.

questions.q: Which guerrilla general of 1857 was a close associate of Nana Saheb?
questions.explanation: Tatya Tope, one of the ablest rebel commanders, was a close associate of Nana Saheb.

questions.q: After seizing Kanpur in 1857, Nana Saheb proclaimed himself to which hereditary Maratha office?
questions.explanation: After capturing Kanpur in 1857, Nana Saheb proclaimed himself Peshwa, the hereditary chief minister's office of the Maratha Confederacy.

questions.q: The 1857 uprising Nana Saheb joined was directed against the rule of whom?
questions.explanation: The revolt of 1857 was directed against the rule of the British East India Company.

questions.q: Near which town did Nana Saheb maintain his residence before the revolt?
questions.explanation: Nana Saheb lived at Bithur, near Kanpur, the seat granted to the exiled Peshwa's family.

questions.q: Who was Nana Saheb's trusted secretary and emissary, sent to England to plead his pension case?
questions.explanation: Azimullah Khan served as Nana Saheb's secretary and travelled to England to argue for the pension.

questions.q: Which British officer commanded the besieged garrison that surrendered to Nana Saheb at Kanpur in June 1857?
questions.explanation: General Sir Hugh Wheeler commanded the entrenchment at Kanpur that capitulated to Nana Saheb's forces.

questions.q: The annual pension dispute central to Nana Saheb's grievance had been granted to Baji Rao II after which event?
questions.explanation: Baji Rao II received his pension after his defeat and abdication following the Third Anglo-Maratha War (1817-1818).

questions.q: Which British general's force advanced to relieve Kanpur, defeating Nana Saheb's army in 1857?
questions.explanation: General Henry Havelock's column marched on Kanpur and defeated Nana Saheb's forces in 1857.

questions.q: Nana Saheb's nephew, also a leader in the revolt, was known by what name?
questions.explanation: Rao Sahib, a nephew of Nana Saheb, was an active leader during the Kanpur revolt and afterward.

questions.q: Which neighbouring uprising's leader, the Begum of Awadh, fought in the same broad theatre as Nana Saheb in 1857?
questions.explanation: Begum Hazrat Mahal led the revolt in nearby Awadh, in the same region as Nana Saheb's Kanpur rising.

questions.q: Where is Nana Saheb most often reported to have fled after the collapse of the revolt?
questions.explanation: He is most commonly reported to have escaped toward Nepal, though his fate was never confirmed.

questions.q: The Doctrine of Lapse, resented by many 1857 leaders, was associated with which Governor-General?
questions.explanation: Lord Dalhousie's Doctrine of Lapse and refusal of adopted heirs' claims fuelled grievances like Nana Saheb's.

questions.q: Nana Saheb later joined which other rebel leader in the campaigns around Gwalior in 1858?
questions.explanation: Nana Saheb's cause continued through Tatya Tope's campaigns in central India, including around Gwalior in 1858.

questions.q: Which river town near Kanpur saw heavy fighting connected to Nana Saheb's revolt?
questions.explanation: Fatehpur, between Allahabad and Kanpur, saw fighting as Havelock advanced against Nana Saheb's forces.

questions.q: Nana Saheb's revolt at Kanpur is most closely linked to which year-defining wider event?
questions.explanation: His rebellion was one of the central episodes of the broader Revolt of 1857.

questions.q: Nana Saheb captured Kanpur after the British garrison surrendered in which month and year?
questions.explanation: The Kanpur garrison under Wheeler surrendered to Nana Saheb in June 1857.

questions.q: Nana Saheb's adoptive father Baji Rao II died in which year, triggering the pension dispute?
questions.explanation: Baji Rao II died in 1851, after which the Company refused to continue the pension to Nana Saheb.

questions.q: The British relieving force first recaptured Kanpur from Nana Saheb's forces in which month of 1857?
questions.explanation: Havelock's column recaptured Kanpur in July 1857 after defeating Nana Saheb's army.

questions.q: Which annual pension figure, around 8 lakh rupees, was at the heart of Nana Saheb's claim against the Company?
options: The Peshwa pension to Baji Rao II
questions.explanation: The disputed sum was the roughly eight-lakh-rupee annual pension that had been paid to Baji Rao II as the deposed Peshwa.

questions.q: Nana Saheb's emissary Azimullah Khan reportedly travelled through which other country en route, observing the Crimean War?
questions.explanation: Azimullah Khan is reported to have passed through the Crimea region, observing the war involving Russia and Turkey, during his European travels.

questions.q: After the fall of Kanpur, Nana Saheb's forces were finally driven out in a second British action in which late month of 1857?
questions.explanation: The Second Battle of Cawnpore in late November to December 1857 secured British control against renewed rebel pressure.

questions.q: Which contemporary British author wrote influential accounts that shaped Western memory of the Kanpur episode under Nana Saheb?
questions.explanation: George Otto Trevelyan's 'Cawnpore' (1865) was a widely read British account of the events at Kanpur.

questions.q: Repeated but unverified British reports of Nana Saheb being sighted continued for decades, mostly placing him in or near which Himalayan kingdom?
questions.explanation: For years after 1857 unconfirmed reports placed Nana Saheb in the forests and hills of Nepal.
